text:
Fun fact I learned recently reading about the history of Rome:

The word “otium” means roughly “to have control of one’s time.” The word for business — “negotium” — means “to not have control of one’s time.”

It’s right there in the English word too, but it seems like we forget that.

raw.full_text:
Fun fact I learned recently reading about the history of Rome:

The word “otium” means roughly “to have control of one’s time.” The word for business — “negotium” — means “to not have control of one’s time.”

It’s right there in the English word too, but it seems like we forget that.

text:
This is a somewhat counterintuitive metaphor, but very interesting to think about.

We talk about the autonomic nervous system as having branches which extend from the brainstem and spinal cord into the organs. 

The roots, therefore, are in the former: the brainstem and spinal cord.

Our organs are our leaves.

text:
Technically no, because I’m not really deep into Zen. I grew up around it so I know sort of superficially what’s involved and some of the ideas, but I haven’t myself directly or seriously engaged with koans.

However, I would say that other ways that I tend to think, experiences I’ve had and practices that I work with, kind of put me in a similar position of having to confront things that intellectually do not coincide with each other. That’s pretty normal for me and how I try to live. And so in that sense, yes, I definitely have sat with — I’ll call them — contradictions or paradoxes or even hypocrisies within myself and the world such that they break me open, for sure.

One thing that comes to mind actually is related to an area I’ve been learning a lot about recently: the history of Rome, and as a part of that, the history of the Catholic Church. In a way, the Catholic Church itself (and probably any religion, once it becomes an institution) seems a bit like a koan.

I may very well be leaning too much into the definition of koan as paradox, so forgive me for that!

text:
I won’t claim that this is what’s prompting that from other people, but I know for myself, when I have a question, there is usually a whole context behind it. A story, if you will, that brings me to the point where I am asking that particular question.

For example, I just got off a call with a reading group I’m a part of. And on today’s call, I asked the opening question. If someone had asked me why I was asking that particular question, there’s a lot I could say. There’s all sorts of stuff I could point to in the text, and the ways that those things strike me as important, that prompted me to be especially curious about this particular idea. Like, “given that the author says X, Y, and Z, what does he mean by…?”

text:
Resurfacing this recording largely as a reminder to myself, but would love to hear your thoughts as well. 

“What does it mean to think for yourself?”

Even before ChatGPT came out, it was always a challenge for me whenever I had some question or curiosity pop into mind. 

My preferred way to engage with a question or curiosity like that is to write about it or to go for a walk and talk through it out loud with a voice recorder. That’s my ideal. That, to me, is to truly think for myself about my own questions. 

But even when I just had Google, it was so compelling to just search my question and then spend a whole bunch of time opening a million tabs, halway read some of them, but never really think things through for myself in a significant way (or at least to the degree that to me feels important to do).

So now, with all these new and incredible tools at our disposal, it’s that same dynamic, but amplified 1,000,000%

text:
Part of this is me trying to consistently remind myself that the answers aren’t always outside of me. That when it comes to some particular question or curiosity that I may have, I myself may be able to figure it out. I may have the experience and knowledge and unique perspective that allows me to answer my own questions. 

And when it’s so easy to go to Google or ChatGPT, it becomes very easy to just totally forget that I myself can be a source of my own wisdom. 

Plus then the whole cognitive/emotional/psychological/etc skillset of thinking things through for oneself just begins to atrophy more and more. And I don’t want that.

text:
I agree it’s not quite an education, problem, if by education we mean information availability. Something I’ve seen in other people, and can imagine if I were only just beginning to contemplate learning, is just not knowing where to start. I think what would be the most helpful is to define constraints. Or, put another way, start by getting clear on the goal and scope. 

To someone starting from scratch, I’d suggest just pick something simple that you genuinely are interested in and enjoy and learn how to cook that. 

If you don’t start specific and simple, it quickly spins into a potentially quite overwhelming situation with a lot of moving parts. There are so many possibilities for what you could learn to cook, along with the complexity of sourcing ingredients and making sure that they don’t go bad on you, and all of the micro skills and processes involved in preparing foods, plus having appropriate equipment and having to deal with dishes. And then of course, different people like to learn in different ways. Some people prefer to follow recipes, while other people like to understand the underlying principles and techniques such that they can improvise. Etc etc…

text:
One day I would like to go back to the beginning and study the canon of direct-response marketing and copywriting. 

When I did it the first time I was so young, fresh out of college, with no experience of actually trying to sell and persuade at a high level, and with no explicit knowledge about relationship dynamics, or the nervous system and trauma.

I think it would be fascinating to re-encounter the basic teachings of persuasion and sales, but with a point of view built on 10+ years working in the industry, almost as much time studying psychology and therapy, and years of actively working with people on understanding and healing their relationship to money.

There are so many unquestioned assumptions in marketing about relationships, ethics, desire and success.

Learning how to promote yourself and make more money is obviously one benefit of studying this stuff, but I think there’s way, way more depth to explore and benefit from.

text:
Martin Heidegger, who followed Husserl, wrote early on that all concepts and distinctions depend upon a more basic kind of understanding that already exists in all human living and practice. It is that felt, "moody" kind of understanding with which we create our situations, the implicit understanding with which we go about acting, trying, going-for, and avoiding. It is an understanding which, he said, "reaches much farther than cognition."

— Eugene Gendlin

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
When you hold your breath, your body wants and pre-figures the familiar exhaling. But not just exhaling is implied, rather any way to get oxygen, and not just oxygen but anything that would fulfill the function oxygen fulfills, and perhaps not just that function but some other function that would serve to keep the body alive. Implying is always like that, very demandingly exact, but more than a fixed form-in-advance.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
For any Keith Johnstone fans out there, I highly recommend 'Improvisation Provides' by Eugene Gendlin.

"Provide" comes from the Latin "providere" which means to see ahead, to foresee. Improvisation, he argues, is the essential means to access the aliveness that underlies form and to discover oneself freshly in each moment of living.

text:
In German "Wie befinden Sie sich" is ordinary language meaning "How are you?" This "are" fits what Heidegger says of humans: We have the kind of being that is involved in situations, projects and worries. Befindlichkeit is how you are, how you find yourself just now, how you are situated, and it is therefore also your "mood."

— Eugene Gendlin

Citizens at the bottom of the pile were generally held to have brought their misfortunes on themselves. Poverty was due to moral failings. text:
Another point on the initial question you asked:

Just talk about the benefits! Shadow work is the method, the “mechanism.” It isn’t the benefits that the people get from using the method. If you can intrigue people with the benefits, that is what can open them up to considering the mechanism.

There’s nuance here of course, but that’s a general principal in marketing.

text: Did Feldenkrais ever come up in your Alexander Technique training? I took and enjoyed AT classes back in 2018 before I learned about Feldenkrais a year or two later. I ended up resonating more with Feldenkrais and going down that rabbit hole. But it and AT have always seemed extremely close together, and I have always been perplexed at the seeming lack of people talking about them side by side, contrasting between the two. They seem extremely complementary, or even more essentially: that they are working on the same “problem” and doing something very similar, but through a different conceptual lens and consequently with different specific concrete practices.

text:
Question: Why are you choosing to apply to @stjohnscollege?

Answer: I don’t want to sit in a class and take notes from a professor. I want to become engaged in my learning and education. I don’t want to continue on the preset path of high school, college, career without serious examination of my values and purposes with regards to a career. My favorite classes have always been those in which the class discusses a text and the values within the text. I have learned the most in classes through discussion because I’ve had to question my own assumptions, and more ideas are exposed than would be through a lecture. All classes at St. John’s are discussion based. I want to ask, “Why?” I have not liked math in school because it is taught at face value. Yet the first mathematicians were also great philosophers. I want to see the philosophy in mathematics.

text:
I believe there is more to mathematics than personal finances, investment strategies and calculating interest rates. Posters on the wall of my Algebra 2/Trigonometry classroom say Euclid and Pythagoras were great philosophers, but who of us would have known, considering the teacher did not mention them or their contributions? The fact that philosophy is the basis for mathematical problem-solving certainly was not apparent in Algebra I, Geometry, Algebra II or Trigonometry, but it is the fact I most wish to explore and am inspired by.

— @daemonhugger, circa 2010

text:
It is our word, the category, that does not change while the state of the present does change.

Thus we find ourselves using something which, though mediated by its changing content, is constant for us. The ‘Now’ is permanent, while Night and Noon pass away, and it can be at either one at any moment. We call these ‘self-preserving’ words, universals.

raw.full_text:
It is our word, the category, that does not change while the state of the present does change.

Thus we find ourselves using something which, though mediated by its changing content, is constant for us. The ‘Now’ is permanent, while Night and Noon pass away, and it can be at either one at any moment. We call these ‘self-preserving’ words, universals.

text: We find that, in order to represent our experience, the object of our expression must be mediated. The night, which in our experience of it was unmediated, becomes mediated in our saying “Night” just because this word is not the night of our experience, and the word exists even when the present is no longer night.

raw.full_text: We find that, in order to represent our experience, the object of our expression must be mediated. The night, which in our experience of it was unmediated, becomes mediated in our saying “Night” just because this word is not the night of our experience, and the word exists even when the present is no longer night.

text:
Great question. I want to reflect on it more before answering in full.

One thing, which is less about specific takeaways, is that I’m progressively shaping my life and work in such a way that it’s not something I want to retire from.

I don’t think there’s a “right” way to approach retirement, but I do think we don’t spend nearly enough attention on asking what we want from retirement and how we’re relating to the idea.

Retirement is one of the most “default,” long-term paths in many of our lives (in the @p_millerd sense) and also one of the least reflected on.

text:
Other cities, ones particularly honored by the Romans, ranked as civitates liberae: settlements spared the obligation to pay tax. This was the freedom that Nero had granted the entire province of Greece, and that Vespasian had rescinded; and it was a status, of course, that was highly prized.

— @holland_tom

raw.full_text:
Other cities, ones particularly honored by the Romans, ranked as civitates liberae: settlements spared the obligation to pay tax. This was the freedom that Nero had granted the entire province of Greece, and that Vespasian had rescinded; and it was a status, of course, that was highly prized.

— @holland_tom

text:
Jealousy, rivalry, ambition: these were emotions that, like the wildfires that sometimes ripped through forests and fields during the hot summer months, had long been at risk, wherever the Greeks had cities, of blazing out of control. The Romans knew this as well as anyone: for it had been the failure of the various Greek states to swallow their differences and fight as one that had facilitated their subjugation.

— @holland_tom

raw.full_text:

The Romans knew this as well as anyone: for it had been the failure of the various Greek states to swallow their differences and fight as one that had facilitated their subjugation.

text:
The days when the citizens of a Greek city, embroiled in a disagreement with a neighbor, could strap on their armour, pick up their spears, and go marching off to war were long gone. The Roman peace was maintained at the point of a sword. Every governor exercised a strict monopoly of violence. Roman rule might have deprived the Greeks of their liberty, but it had to bestowed on them a condition that they had never enjoyed while they were free: an enduring and universal peace.

— @holland_tom

text:
I am not concerned with the ideological issue, whether we should foretell a future in which computers can replace human intelligence, and whether that would be good, or whether it will become clear that this is impossible, and also, that it would be bad. Rather, I am concerned with the interface between our natural understanding and the realm of logical forms. To become able to handle this interface, we must become able to think on both sides.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
In psychotherapy, too, experiencing is more precise than can be said in common phrases. To refer to such experiencing and express it leads to deeper therapeutic change. Explicating changes it, and leads to renewed dipping and another change-step, and another, to more and more new experience.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
Our bodies imply every next bit of our further living. An action can explicate this implicit further living, and can carry it forward. To explicate in words and in logic are special cases of such further living. So, of course, dipping into a felt sense brings what we want to do or say next.

— Eugene Gendlin

Come now: watch with every palm how 
each single thing becomes apparent. Don't hold 
anything you see as any more of an assurance 
than whatever you hear, or give those loud 
sounds you happen to be hearing preference 
over the sharp tastes on your tongue. And don't 
reject the assurance provided by any other limb 
that offers some passage for perception, but 
perceive how each single thing becomes apparent.

— Empedocles

I recorded the whole thing and dropped the transcript into Claude this morning. Having spent so many years as a direct-response copywriter scripting webinars and long form sales pages, I was curious to see how this particular person approached the pitch.

text:
When I heard about Emma Watson’s “self partnering,” it made me wonder what kind of partner I am to myself.

Do I encourage myself to face my inner demons, and grow from them?

Do I affirm to myself that I am valuable, attractive, and worthy of life and love?

Do I accept the emotions that I experience? Or do I ignore them, get annoyed by them, or shame myself for them?

Do I take care of myself?

Do you?

Do I accept the emotions that I experience? Or do I ignore them, get annoyed by them, or shame myself for them?

Do I take care of myself?

Do you?

text: When it comes down to it, it’s no one else’s responsibility to take care of myself. No one else is responsible for loving me, except me.

text:
The last few days I’ve been staying up past midnight and eating a lot junk food. I can tell that I’m worked up or anxious about something. But instead of making choices to soothe myself, reflect on what I’m feeling, and tend to my health, I deprive myself of sleep and stuff myself with carbs and sweets.

Is this the kind of behavior I’d want my girlfriend to encourage in me? If not, than why am I encouraging it in myself? Why am I not intervening on my own behalf?

text:
A safe and steady human presence willing to be with whatever comes up is a most powerful factor. If we do not try to improve or change anything, if we add nothing, if however bad something is, we only say what we understand exactly, such a response adds our presence and helps clients to stay with and go further into whatever they sense and feel just then. This is perhaps the most important thing that any person helping others needs to know.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
Dilthey held that we never really have the same understanding as the author had. If we understand a work at all, we understand it better than its author did. We must create the author's process out of our own, thereby augmenting both. In our terms we can say that they cross: Some of each becomes implicit in the other. The author's statements do not change, but implicitly they now contain our own experience as well.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
Direct reference is itself a change, and then leads to a further move.

— Eugene Gendlin

raw.full_text:
Direct reference is itself a change, and then leads to a further move.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
The kind of transition I call "direct reference" is itself a kind of symbolizing. It lifts out (creates, finds, synthesizes, differentiates, .....) a "this," which was not a this before. When we seem to find what "was" there, we have actually moved further.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
We must not merely denigrate logic. It has developed enormous sophistication in our time and has brought the wonderful technology that enables many more people to live, and live better than before.

— Eugene Gendlin

raw.full_text:
We must not merely denigrate logic. It has developed enormous sophistication in our time and has brought the wonderful technology that enables many more people to live, and live better than before.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
As we become able to think in the implicit experiential context of science, we can develop ways to bring more than the market to bear on scientific and social policies.

— Eugene Gendlin

raw.full_text:
As we become able to think in the implicit experiential context of science, we can develop ways to bring more than the market to bear on scientific and social policies.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
How can one dismiss logic, just when it is changing everything around us? In the current debates, only one group seems to appreciate the powers of logic, while the other is alone in knowing the limits. These two groups hardly speak to each other. We can hope to develop a society-wide understanding of the power of logical deduction, as well as ways in which we can employ the wider process of human sense-making. They have distinguishable powers. We need to become able to think how they function in each other.

— Eugene Gendlin

We can hope to develop a society-wide understanding of the power of logical deduction, as well as ways in which we can employ the wider process of human sense-making. They have distinguishable powers. We need to become able to think how they function in each other.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
Applying any concept elicits an experiential feedback. We can let our next step of thought come from this experiential feedback, rather than only from the concept. We can think with both conceptual and experiential steps, a "zigzag" which employs both powers. It can make new sense and lead us to modify our concepts, rather than being confined in them or ending in mere contradictions. Experiential thinking moves beyond postmodern "rupture" and contradiction.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
The desire for a single map or system must be given up (why would we want nature to be so poor?), but not truth.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
Contrary to the current view, nature is not arbitrary or invented. It is more orderly than a cognitive system. It is a "responsive order" which gives various, but always more exact, results than could have been constructed or deduced. It leads to an empiricism that is not naive.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
A theory may lead to an experiential detail, but from the detail much can follow which cannot follow from the theory.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
Neither in life nor in philosophy are we limited to rearranging the existing, already-formed things and concepts. We can engage the experiential meanings. We can deliberately employ and expand the vital roles which they perform.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
Philosophy can reopen the old assumptions and conceptual models if we think with our more intricate experiencing as well as with logic. We can think everything more truly if we think it philosophically, that is, with attention to how we think it, and with the critical understanding that no concept, rule, or distinction ever equals experiencing.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
What does it mean to be yourself?

I wrote my senior thesis on that question just over ten years ago while studying the Nicomachean Ethics @stjohnscollege.

As I understand it, one of Aristotle's claims is that happiness (εὐδαιμονία) is the ongoing participation in the activity which is most uniquely you. One’s true nature, if you will.

I recently had the honor of spending close to three hours with @tasshinfogleman on the Reach Truth podcast tracing where that question has taken me 🧵

text:
The chip on my shoulder that became the work

I grew up in Hawai’i with a professor and a midwife as parents. We were not commercially oriented. In fact we had a lot of antagonism toward the world of business and finance. But then at around 11 years old I switched from a Waldorf to a private prep school and suddenly I was surrounded by people with a lot more money.

My dad used to tell me that "we are rich." In retrospect I get what he meant, but at the time it seemed patently false. I even lost some trust in him, because what he was saying contradicted my adolescent understanding of what it meant to have wealth.

This turned into a feeling that I carried with me for many years. That the people with money knew how the world really works, and that I didn’t.

My life since then has been utterly shaped by that experience.

text:
Inside the objectification machine

After St. John's I taught myself direct response copywriting and began working in financial publishing. At its core, my job was to get inside the minds of our readers and speak to them from there. To find and engage with the conversation already happening in their mind.

Our customers were mostly white men of retirement age, largely conservative or libertarian, often financially precarious and bitter. The marketing was largely fear-driven. Huge promises. Aggressive funnels. Internally we called it “churn and burn.”

I had to emotionally vomit into a journal for hours every morning before I could bring myself to start work.

Then in 2016 I read Kahneman. One study hit me hard: people primed with words related to old age in a Scrabble game walked slower down a hallway afterward, without knowing it.

I started asking myself: if that happens from being exposed to ideas they didn't even realize, what does that mean for me, as I spend all day every day trying to live in the minds of our customers?

That's not what I wanted. So I started looking for the exit.

That's not what I wanted. So I started looking for the exit.

text:
Deep Spontaneity

I had been an extremely active kid, but after college I became deeply disembodied. I was spending my days ghostwriting as other people, socially anxious and unable to participate in meetings, going home and processing everything in writing afterward. I hated that. I wanted to be engaged in the moment.

Thanks to @vgr I discovered Impro by Keith Johnstone and had what I can only describe as an intellectually orgasmic experience while reading it in a Starbucks in 2017.

I got into improv. Then I found Meisner acting, which is based on a simple but powerful idea: acting is living truthfully under imaginary circumstances.

I went from one of the most introverted people at St. John's to people thinking I was the most outgoing person in the room. I also learned to cry. Not perform crying. Actually cry. In public. That was huge for me. I had been feeling like I needed to cry for years by that point, but couldn’t.

The whole experience drove home how disembodied I'd become.

I realized I wanted to use my voice more, so I bought a voice recorder and started dictating my journals instead of writing. Talking nonstop for 20-minutes to an hour every morning. The voice gets to things the keyboard doesn't. 
Nowadays I barely write. I think out loud.

Nowadays I barely write. I think out loud.

text:
A Gendlin-shaped question

Eugene Gendlin was a philosopher at the University of Chicago in the 1950s who wrote his dissertation on the question: what is the relationship between symbols and lived experience?

At St. John’s we only had two opportunities for electives (which they call “preceptorials”). I did my second one on the Nicomachean Ethics. The first time, I chose to read The Phenomenology of Perception by Maurice Merleau-Ponty. He was the first philosopher I encountered who took seriously the idea that we are embodied. It was electrifying. Gendlin, I discovered many years later, was explicitly building off of Merleau-Ponty’s work.

The writing teacher I discovered in college (Peter Elbow, from whom I learned freewriting and the power of voice) was influenced by Gendlin and refers to him in his books. The somatic meditation practice I found in 2018 (Somatic Descent, which @the_wilderless has written about) explicitly references Gendlin as a core influence.

By the time I began reading Gendlin in 2019 I realized he'd already been inside much of what I'd gravitated toward over the previous decade. The glove fit the hand perfectly.

@tasshinfogleman talks about vows as emergent, discovered in the dance between you and the world. I think that's what happened. The question I wrote my thesis on emerged almost like a proto-vow. That question and all my ensuing attempts to explore it pointed me to Gendlin.

text:
Focusing as meta-theory

Most people who've heard of Gendlin seem to treat Focusing as one modality among many. As something to add to your toolkit alongside IFS or Somatic Experiencing or CBT.

I think that’s the wrong way to look at it. To me, Gendlin built the meta-theory behind all therapeutic work. His process philosophy (the “philosophy of the implicit” as he calls it) is the framework within which every modality either works or doesn't.

At UChicago Gendlin teamed up with Carl Rogers and began asking the question: what is actually happening when therapy is successful? As @nosilverv described in his Reach Truth interview, after analyzing thousands of hours of therapy recordings, Gendlin and Rogers realized that the clients who got better were the ones who stumbled. Who tried to say something, found it wasn't quite right, and tried again.

The people who were successful in therapy were checking their words against their bodies. Everyone else was recounting stories and insights they already knew. Their words were articulate but, in a sense, empty.

Focusing is the essential sub-process that allows you to check whether something is genuinely happening, whether you're spinning wheels intellectually or actually moving. You can do CBT, IFS, shadow work, psychoanalysis — whatever — in a way that never actually touches you. Or you can bring focusing to any approach and check.

Peter Levine credited Gendlin for giving him the language on which he built Somatic Experiencing. I think that’s an understatement.

text:
Goldman as shadow work

As I mentioned, I grew up in a family and culture with real antagonism toward institutional finance. But already in 2020 I knew I wanted to do something like the work I do now. And if I was going to work seriously in the domain of money, it would be disingenuous of me to not confront my biases head on.

So after years of financial copywriting, I joined Goldman Sachs. I wanted to go inside and find out what was actually there, rather than maintaining a projection from the outside.

I spent about three years inside. For support, I started working with an incredible coach to help process everything that came up for me as I immersed myself in that world. I made a sort of pact with myself. I wouldn’t leave until I’d made peace with being there. Until I could laugh like the Buddha at the ridiculousness of working in corporate finance.

In many ways I achieved that. Not perfectly, but more or less. And then I remembered there was something I wanted to be doing way more with my time. Something that felt more like a calling.

text:
How Money Mapping began

In early 2022 I started something called the Parasocial Podcast. You can find some episodes on my Youtube channel. I’d take long walks playing @visakanv’s videos out loud on my phone with a voice recorder running. I’d pause the video and engage with what he was saying, treating it like a conversation, similar to how we learned to engage with the Great Books at St. Johns.

Visa made a a number of videos about money and marketing. I took one of them – about not wanting to be a sell out – and made a map of it. I shared it with him, then improvised a video explaining why money was a such big area of focus for me and how Money Mapping was my way of helping people explore and heal their own relationship to it. I pinned it to my profile and within a year I was booked out nine months.

This work draws on a lineage of more names than I can recount here. But a HUGE one came in 2020 when @RichDecibels shared an article by @tomnixon that introduced me to Peter Koenig's moneywork. Shortly after that I joined a 3-day Money workshop by @LifeCentered1 which was based on Peter’s work. Then I joined another multi-month course with @AhojNadjeschda based on his work. I went so deep into the program it took me almost three years to finish.

text:
Money as a final frontier

I've been watching this pattern online for years. People who have done serious inner work — IFS, Core Transformation, Focusing, somatic practice, meditation – often have gone deep in many of the richest territories of human life: love, sex, the body, death, meaning, etc.

But almost never money.

The therapy world generally avoids it (though that’s changing slowly with Financial Therapy). The spiritual world avoids it as something dirty and materialistic. And then the finance world avoids the emotional, psychological, spiritual and relational human dimensions, instead prioritizing numbers, logic and spreadsheets.

Very few people are looking at economic experience from the inside.

But something I’ve come to believe more and more is that change is a byproduct of that kind of awareness. People often start with the frame that there’s something wrong, something irrational or nonsensical about themselves that needs to be changed. My take is that the real work is attention and recognition. Witnessing. Seeing the truth of the existing reality and the larger context in which these particular phenomena are taking place.

The avoidance, the anxiety, the self-contradictions are real. Something true is already being expressed. Because ultimately our thoughts, feelings and actions are accurate responses to real conditions. And the work is to understand the conditions.

I try to create a space where people can finally get curious: what do I actually think about money? What did I learn from my parents and culture? What do I genuinely want? How am I treating money? And how is it treating me?

Because usually we don’t actually want money itself. And it’s often not money itself that’s blocking us.

But something I’ve come to believe more and more is that change is a byproduct of that kind of awareness. People often start with the frame that there’s something wrong, something irrational or nonsensical about themselves that needs to be changed. My take is that the real work is attention and recognition. Witnessing. Seeing the truth of the existing reality and the larger context in which these particular phenomena are taking place.

The avoidance, the anxiety, the self-contradictions are real. Something true is already being expressed. Because ultimately our thoughts, feelings and actions are accurate responses to real conditions. And the work is to understand the conditions.

I try to create a space where people can finally get curious: what do I actually think about money? What did I learn from my parents and culture? What do I genuinely want? How am I treating money? And how is it treating me?

Because usually we don’t actually want money itself. And it’s often not money itself that’s blocking us.

text:
Long-term thinking as nervous system regulation

I had a cancer scare last year.

For a week before I got to the doctor I was wracked with anxiety and spiraling. But at one point I sat down and thought about what I want this work to become over the next ten or twenty years. And suddenly the fear diminished. I hadn’t minimized or suppressed it. I had made a space large enough to hold it.

Remember the Meisner definition of acting? “Acting is living truthfully under imaginary circumstances.” I’ve realized that can be applied to life in general. 

A compelling future doesn't have to be held as a thing you must bring to fruition. A compelling future creates a container in which you live now, today.

What if instead of thinking in terms of the next twelve months, you're thinking 25 years ahead? And not just ahead but backwards. Reflecting on where you were 25 years ago and where you want to be 25 years from today. Suddenly you’re living in a huge container of time. 

One of my long-time teachers @Irene_Lyon talks about expanding somatic capacity as a fundamental part of nervous system regulation and trauma healing. By developing more space within the body – greater interoception, more mobility, less tension, etc – stored stress begins to rise to the surface and resolve itself spontaneously.

That’s building capacity along the somatic dimension. But I’ve been asking myself: along what other dimensions can I increase my capacity? And one of the most interesting ways I’ve been experimenting with that is time. Playing with longer-term thinking, both backwards and forwards.

text:
With the coming of industry everything changed. The man went to work in the factory, the wife stayed home all day, the kid went to school. The woman went to see her friends, and they were all seeing different people. The people that the man met in the factory were not the parents of the kids that went to school with his kid, and they were not related to the friends that the woman had in the town. That split up everything and made all the conditions of living different.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
It required a human development for people to run machinery. They had to be taught how to read instructions, they had to be taught how to be careful with powerful machinery, they had to be taught to be there at 8:00 in the morning, because the whole factory can't work unless everybody is there, because they are working on the assembly line and when two guys are missing the whole thing stops. So everybody had to buy a watch, everybody had to learn numbers. There was this tremendous development of people, inside and outside.

— Eugene Gendlin

text:
It required a human development for people to run machinery. — Eugene Gendlin

— Eugene Gendlin @Casa_Tilo
